description: Cross-session memory persistence using vector databases to recall past user interactions, successful tool uses, and learned preferences.
Agents can autonomously search a registry for new tools/APIs during runtime if existing tools fail to solve the task.
Agents can directly ingest and reason over screenshots, UI elements, and diagrams without OCR middleware.
Manager agents can autonomously break down complex goals and spawn sub-agents with specific contexts.
Cyclic, stateful workflows that allow agents to loop, reflect, and retry failed actions based on a persistent graph state.
Routing user requests to the most appropriate specialized agent based on vector similarity of the query intent.
Configurable pause points where agents request explicit human approval before taking high-risk actions (e.g., executing code, spending money).
Agents critique their own outputs against a rubric and iteratively refine them before finalizing.
A shared state object (like a virtual whiteboard) where multiple agents can read/write intermediate results concurrently.
Tracking which tools are most effective for specific tasks and proactively suggesting them to agents.
Native ability to process voice inputs and generate voice outputs for accessibility and hands-free operation.
Real-time streaming of agent thought processes and outputs to the UI for better perceived latency.
Forcing two agents with opposing personas to debate a topic to arrive at a more robust, unbiased conclusion.
A secure, isolated environment (e.g., Docker) where agents can write, execute, and debug code safely.
Agents autonomously extract entities and relationships from documents to build a structured knowledge graph.
Hard limits and tracking for LLM token usage per agent, per task, or per session to control costs.
Framework automatically optimizes agent prompts and tool descriptions based on few-shot examples and evaluation metrics.
Algorithms (like summarization or key-value extraction) to condense long conversation histories before passing to the LLM.
Agents interacting with desktop applications or web browsers via accessibility APIs or visual coordinate mapping.
Agnostic LLM backend supporting OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and local models (Ollama/vLLM) interchangeably.
Agents actively decide *when* to search a database, *what* query to use, and *how* to synthesize the retrieved chunks.
Restricting specific agents or workflows from accessing sensitive tools (e.g., only Finance Agent can access Stripe API).
Visualizing the exact decision tree, prompt variables, and tool outputs that led to an agent's final answer.
Agents correctly utilizing a novel API or tool based purely on its OpenAPI specification and description, without prior examples.
Searching across text, images, and audio simultaneously using unified embedding spaces (e.g., CLIP).
Agents dispatching long-running tasks (like web scraping or model training) and pausing until a webhook callback is received.
A no-code interface allowing users to quickly wrap an HTTP endpoint or Python script into an agent-compatible tool.
Defining specific tones, constraints, and background knowledge for agents to act as specific roles (e.g., 'Grumpy Unix Sysadmin').
Caching LLM responses based on semantic similarity of the prompt to save costs and reduce latency on repetitive queries.
Detecting stuck or endlessly looping agents and automatically terminating/restarting their execution graph.
Native ingestion of PDF, DOCX, CSV, and PPTX files into markdown or structured JSON for agent consumption.
Defining complex multi-agent interaction graphs using simple, version-controllable YAML files instead of code.
Agents sharing generalized learnings across different user organizations without exposing sensitive PII.
Specialized agents capable of navigating websites, handling JavaScript rendering, and extracting structured data.
Agents capable of analyzing video feeds frame-by-frame to answer questions about events or actions occurring in the footage.
Built-in testing frameworks to evaluate agent accuracy, tool use success rate, and adherence to constraints.
Using a smaller, fast model (or heuristics) to classify user intent before invoking expensive, reasoning-heavy agent workflows.
Integration with Key Vaults (AWS Secrets Manager, Hashicorp) to dynamically inject API keys into tools only when needed.
Agents returning Flutter components based on the task (e.g., rendering a custom chart instead of just text).
Strict enforcement of agent outputs to match a predefined JSON schema before allowing the workflow to proceed.
Memory systems that decay old information or prioritize recent events automatically.
Tools enabling agents to read blockchain state, sign transactions (with HITL), and deploy smart contracts.
Agents capable of reasoning over 3D point clouds or spatial data for robotic control or CAD manipulation.
Ensuring one organization's agents, memory, and tool configurations cannot leak or interact with another's in a SaaS environment.
Automatically retrying a failed LLM call using a different provider (e.g., fallback from OpenAI to Anthropic if rate-limited).
A developer-focused agent that lives in the terminal, capable of executing bash commands and reading local files.
Using Neo4j or similar graph DBs to store complex, highly-connected facts about an organization.
Native integration to read, draft, and send emails or Slack messages directly from agent workflows.
Agents waking up based on external events (e.g., a new GitHub issue, a cron schedule) rather than direct user prompts.
Middleware that automatically strips Personally Identifiable Information (PII) before sending context to external LLM providers.
